                    PETITION FOR WRIT OF ADMINISTRATIVE MANDATE, ORDINARY MANDATE,  
                                      AND DECLARATOR RELIEF                         
   .DTL,SETAICOSSA&                                                                 
   SAMIS                                                                            
    WALTASYENROTTA                                                                  
                                                              ELECTRONICALLY        
              SIMAS & ASSOCIATES, LTD.                          F I L E D           
              Steven L. Simas (State Bar No. 150901)                                
                                                              Superior Court of California,
              Ryan M. Keever (State Bar No. 301204)            County of San Francisco
              Frances E. Heredia (State Bar No. 352103)         08/30/2024          
              7355 Morro Road, Suite 101                      Clerk of the Court    
              Atascadero, California 93422                     BY: AUSTIN LAM       
              805.547.9300 (telephone)                               Deputy Clerk   
              805.547.9302 (facsimile)                                              
              info@simasgovlaw.com                                                  
              Attorneys for Petitioner Jialiang Dong                                
                           SUPERIOR COURT OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA                
                                   COUNTY  OF SAN FRANCISCO                         
                                                                 CPF-24-518677      
              JIALIANG DONG, an individual;   Case No.:                             
                         Petitioners,         PETITION FOR WRIT OF                  
                                              ADMINISTRATIVE MANDATE,  WRIT OF      
                    vs.                       ORDINARY  MANDATE, AND REQUEST        
                                              FOR DECLARATORY  RELIEF               
              UNIVERSITY OF SAN FRANCISCO                                           
              SCHOOL  OF LAW,                 (Code Civ. Proc.,§§ 1094.5, 1085, and 1060)
                         Respondent.                                                
                                              Judge:                                
                                              Dept.:                                
                  Petitioner Jialiang Dong,(“Mr. Dong” or “Petitioner”) hereby respectfully petitions this
              Court to issue a writ of ordinary mandate pursuant to Section 1085, administrative mandate under
              Section 1094.5, and declaratory relief under Section 1060of the California Code of Civil Procedure
              directing the University of San Francisco – School of Law (“USF”) to lift the suspension on Mr.
              Dong on his schooling imposed by the USF’s ruling in the academic complaint investigation and
              subsequent hearing, of which his appeal was denied on August 2, 2024. 
                                    JURISDICTION AND VENUE                          
                  1.   This Court has jurisdiction to consider this petition pursuant to California Code of
              Civil Procedure § 1085 and § 1094.5.                                  
                  2.   The final rulingentered against Petitioner was at the University of San Francisco –

                                             -2 -                                   
                                                                                    
                    PETITION FOR WRIT OF ADMINISTRATIVE MANDATE, ORDINARY MANDATE,  
                                      AND DECLARATOR RELIEF                         
   .DTL,SETAICOSSA&                                                                 
   SAMIS                                                                            
    WALTASYENROTTA                                                                  
              School of Law, located at 2130 Fulton Street, San Francisco, California94117,in San Francisco
              County and the Superior Court of the State of California, County of San Francisco is thus the
              location where the dispute arose. San FranciscoCounty is the appropriate venue. (Code Civ. Proc.,
              § 393.)                                                               
                                           PARTIES                                  
                  3.   Petitioner Mr.Dongis aresident of California and a third-year law student at USF.
                  4.   Respondent USFis now, and at all times mentioned in this petition has been, a
              private, nonprofit universityestablished in 1855, and theSchool of Law was founded in 1912. It is
              accredited by the American Bar Association and is a member of the Association of American Law
              Schools.1                                                             
                                    GENERAL  ALLEGATIONS                            
                  5.   For nearly threeyears, Mr. Dong has been alaw student at USF. He is paying tuition
              of $27,500 per semester, earning his education, and working to becomean attorney in the State of
              California. During his time at USF Mr. Donghas received the Dean Scholarship for academic
              excellence.                                                           
                  6.   On December7, 2023, Mr. Dong submitted a paper as a final assignment in a
              required USF course, Art Law, to USF Professor Karren Shorofsky (“Professor Shorofsky”)
                  7.   ProfessorShorofsky ran a “plagiarism detection software”on Mr. Dong’s paper and,
              after reviewing the “similarity index” determined that the failure to contain borrowed text within
              quotation marks, while the referenced materials were cited in the bibliography, was a potential
              violation of the USF Honor Code. (Committee Report issued June 4, 2024, at p.1)
                  8.   Professor Shorofsky reported Mr. Dong to Associate Dean Amy Flynn (“Dean
              Flynn”) via email for a potential violation of the USF law school honor code and accused Mr. Dong
              of plagiarism. Professor Shorofsky enclosed the similarity report as the basis for the potential
              violation. (Committee Report issued June 4, 2024, at p.2)             
              1 https://www.usfca.edu/law/our-difference/quick-facts

                                             -3 -                                   
                                                                                    
                    PETITION FOR WRIT OF ADMINISTRATIVE MANDATE, ORDINARY MANDATE,  
                                      AND DECLARATOR RELIEF                         
   .DTL,SETAICOSSA&                                                                 
   SAMIS                                                                            
    WALTASYENROTTA                                                                  
                  9.   Mr. Dong replied to Professor Shorofsky’s email, noting that he did cite the author,
              apologizing for the problems leading to the misattribution, acknowledging his lack of understanding
              of the rules related to plagiarism in this context, from his prior legal writing courses. (Id.)
                  10.  On January 9, 2024, Mr. Dong met with Dean Flynn to discuss the allegation and try
              and resolve the matterinformally, but no resolution was reached. (Id.)
                  11.  On February 5, 2024, Dean Flynn emailed Mr. Dong an offer of settlement that
              included terms of placing an honor violation letter in Mr. Dong’s USF personnel file, notice that the
              school will turn over the letter to the California State Bar upon for the moral character application,
              however, the letter will be removed from Mr. Dong’s file if he complete’ s his JD without further
              incident and no other honor code violations. Mr. Dong was also instructed to re-write the final
              paper, with a loss of 2/3 credit.                                     
                  12.  Mr. Dong met with Dean Flynn on February 16, 2024, to discuss the allegation and
              informal offer of resolution. Mr. Dong was informed that the offer was conditioned upon him
              signing a formal release, discharging USF for any claims by Mr. Dong related to the honor code
              violation. Mr. Dong rejected the informal resolution.                 
                  13.  In February 2024, Dean Flynn appointed investigators USF Professor Lara Bazelon
              and USF student Jackie Nemanish (collectively “investigators”) to investigate Mr. Dong’s matter.
              (Id.)                                                                 
                  14.  Mr. Dong was notified of the investigation on February 27, 2024, citing his alleged
              violation of USF Honor Code Section 4.1(b)(3). (Id.)                  
                  15.  Mr. Dong stated that he provided a written response on Feb 28, 2024 and met with
              the investigators via zoom on March 12.                               
                  16.  On March 25, 2024, attorney Negin Yamini noticed her representation of Mr. Dong
              to the investigators and the Honor Committee. (Id.)                   
                  17.  On May 14, 2024, Mr. Dong was represented by counsel Ms. Yamini at hearing
              before the Honor Committee where the testimony of Professor Shorofsky, Dean Flynn, and Mr.
              Dong was heard, and the USF investigators provided argument. (Id.)

                                             -4 -                                   
                                                                                    
                    PETITION FOR WRIT OF ADMINISTRATIVE MANDATE, ORDINARY MANDATE,  
                                      AND DECLARATOR RELIEF                         
   .DTL,SETAICOSSA&                                                                 
   SAMIS                                                                            
    WALTASYENROTTA                                                                  
                  18.  Evidence presented at hearing consisted:                     
                       Mr. Dong’s paper after it was run through Turnitinby USF; Mr. Dong’s paper after it
                       was run through Turnitin by a USF Librarian (3-25-24); Mary LaFrance’s article;
                       Mr. Dong’s paper annotated by Professor Shorofsky; Professor Shorofsky’s syllabus;
                       Mr. Dong’s written response to the allegations; Mr. Dong’s March 13, 2024 email;
                       email exchanges contained in Exhibits A-D of the complaint; Dean Flynn’s Honor
                       Code Presentation; Honor Code Agreement signed by Mr. Dong (August 16, 2022);
                       Mr. Dong’s LRWA paper with comments from Professor Edith Ho. 
                       (Id – at footnote No. 2.)                                    
              After hearing, Mr. Dong’s counsel also provided a submission in his support.
                  19.  Mr. Dong proffered his mid-term paper from the USF required course, Legal
              Research, Writing, and Analysis (“LRWA”), taught by USF Professor Edith Ho (“Professor Ho”).
              In the comments from Professor Ho noted, “You are quoting from the editor notes of Campbell.
              This is not good. You need to quote only from the court opinion because the editor notes are not
              primary authorities. Never quote when it is not actual law.” (Id at p. 4, emphasis added)
                  20.  Mr. Dong explained that English was not his first language, he was raised in another
              country, and that he mistakenly took his legal writing teacher, Professor Ho’s verbatim comment to
              mean he wasn’t permitted to put quotes around anything except court opinions and statutes. (Id. at p.
              4-5)                                                                  
                  21.  In furtherance Mr. Dong’s arguments, he also explained that he had made
              unsuccessful attempts to meet with Professor Shorofsky about the paper’s subject prior to the
              paper’s submission; along with the misinterpretation of guidance given to him by Professor Ho, Mr.
              Dong had also understood advisement from research librarian USF Professor Michael Muehe to say
              that there was no need to Bluebook the research paper. (Id. at p. 5)  
                  22.  The Committee’s own report said that they ran the paper through the plagiarism
              software twice, with varying results and without citing to an actual threshold for the standard of
              evidence that “raises concerns.” (Id. at p. 1 – footnote No. 1)       
                  23.  Disregarding Mr. Dong’s defenses, the Honor Committee held that “the evidence
              demonstrates that Mr. Dong was repeatedly and specifically apprised of what plagiarism means and

                                             -5 -                                   
                                                                                    
                    PETITION FOR WRIT OF ADMINISTRATIVE MANDATE, ORDINARY MANDATE,  
                                      AND DECLARATOR RELIEF                         
   .DTL,SETAICOSSA&                                                                 
   SAMIS                                                                            
    WALTASYENROTTA                                                                  
              how to properly attribute unoriginal ideas” by virtue of Professor Shorofsky’s syllabus, Mr. Dong’s
              attendance of the LRWA course, and his abilitytoBluebook.2 (Id. at p. 4)
                  24.  Ultimately, in the USF Honor Committee’s report they used Mr. Dong’s intelligence
              against him, stating that he was “bright” and shouldhave understood the context of Professor Ho’s
              grading notedue to his prior education.3 (Id. at p. 4-5)              
                  25.  When Mr. Dong’s counsel argued that there was no proof that he intended to violate
              the Honor Code, the Committee’s Report notes that they don’t need proof per Section 4.1(b)(3)(Id.
              at p. 5) and says Mr. Dong failed to meet his burden of proof of a preponderance of evidence that he
              inadvertently violated the Honor Code (Id. at p. 3-4).                
                  26.  The Committee Report found the charge “merited” and suspended Mr. Dong for the
              Fall semester of 2024, placing him on disciplinary probation, and a formal reprimand in his
              permanent record, but not his transcript. Further the decision requires Mr. Dong to complete the
              Indiana University Plagiarism Tutorials and Certification Test, and to redraft the subject paper
              under the terms of specifically assigned close reading and meeting with the Associate Dean or
              designee on the subjects of Ethical Use of Borrowed Materials, plagiarism, and proper attribution in
              legal writing. Additionally, he will be given no credit for the entire Art Law course.
                          PETITION FOR WRIT OF TRADITIONAL MANDATE                  
                                      (Code Civ. Proc., § 1085)                     
                  27.  Petitioner references paragraphs 1 through 26above and incorporates them herein by
              reference as though fully set forth.                                  
                  28.  Petitioner has a beneficial interest in the outcome of the proceedings. (Code of Civil
              Procedure, § 1086.) An adverse result could permanently affect his professional reputation, potential
              licensure with the California State Bar, and ability to get future work.
                  29.  Petitioner is seeking to compel Respondent to act pursuant to statutory and other law.
              2 The LRWA course and Blue-booking were both taught by Professor Ho who was the author of the note that Mr. Dong
              cites as the source of his confusion.                                 
              3 Mr. Dong’s background was in Economics, not in the language arts, history, or philosophy fields that many law
              students hail from.

                                             -6 -                                   
                                                                                    
                    PETITION FOR WRIT OF ADMINISTRATIVE MANDATE, ORDINARY MANDATE,  
                                      AND DECLARATOR RELIEF                         
   .DTL,SETAICOSSA&                                                                 
   SAMIS                                                                            
    WALTASYENROTTA                                                                  
              (Code of Civil Procedure, §1085(a).) Respondent USFis an inferior tribunal, board,or person and
              the performance of the act(s) that the law specially enjoins, and has a duty resulting from an office,
              trust, or stations.                                                   
                  30.  Respondent has a clear, present, ministerial, and legal duty to only suspend a student
              wherethere is clear legal authority to do so.                         
                  31.  Petitioner contends that a suspension is not valid because the consequence is overly
              harsh and punishment for Mr. Dong seeking the administrative remedy rather than accepting USF’s
              information resolution, which required Mr. Dong to forgo any future claims against USF.
                  32.  The USFis obligated to perform in a legally prescribed manner when these given
              facts exist and to perform these acts without exercise of discretion or opinion. This petition seeks to
              correct USF’s abuse of discretion or acts in excess of its legal powers, including correcting actions
              that are arbitrary, capriciously, or entirely without evidentiary support, and those that did not
              conform to law.                                                       
                  33.  An action in ordinary mandamus is proper where the claim is that an agency has
              failed to act as required by law.                                     
                  34.  Ordinary mandamus lies because the USF’s actions were arbitrary, capricious, or
              entirely lacking in evidentiary support, contrary to established public policy, unlawful, procedurally
              unfair, or the agency failed to follow the procedure and give the notices the law requires. (Code of
              Civil Procedure, §1085; City of Scotts Valley v. County of Santa Cruz (2011) 200 Cal.App.4th 97;
              Jefferson v. Compton Unified School Dist. (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 32.)  
                  35.  Respondent abused its discretion by suspending Petitioner from courses without
              evidentiary support to do so and in a manner that is against public policy.
                  36.  Respondent’s abuse of discretion has caused and threatens to cause Petitioner harm
              in that (1) it failed to provide due process, proper notice of the allegations against Petitioner, and
              permitted unnecessary witnesses to be present during testimony.       
                  37.  Furthermore, USF claims that the Petitioner failed to meet the burden of proof
              established (preponderance of evidence) that he did not intend violate the Honor Code by leaving

                                             -7 -                                   
                                                                                    
                    PETITION FOR WRIT OF ADMINISTRATIVE MANDATE, ORDINARY MANDATE,  
                                      AND DECLARATOR RELIEF                         
   .DTL,SETAICOSSA&                                                                 
   SAMIS                                                                            
    WALTASYENROTTA                                                                  
              quotation marks out ofhis paper—this is untrue, as established above. 
                  38.  Finally, USFhas failed to establish that there was an intentional failure to attribute
              the information perpetratedby Mr. Dong who not only cited the article from which he used text in
              his bibliography, but also completely took responsibility for his misunderstanding and provided an
              examples of his ignorance of the rules through simple misinterpretation of guidance from multiple,
              credible sources (the legal writing teacher and the research librarian, Professors Ho and Muehe,
              respectively).                                                        
                  39.  Petitioner has no plain, speedy, or adequate remedy at law regarding Respondent’s
              failures to adhere to the law, other than the relief sought by this Petition for Writ of Mandate. (Code
              of Civil Procedure, § 1086.)                                          
                              WRIT OF ADMINISTRATIVE MANDAMUS                       
                                         (CCP § 1094.5)                             
                  40.  Petitioner incorporates the allegations set forth in paragraphs 1-39 above as fully set
              forth herein.                                                         
                  41.  Petitioner seeks to challenge the validity of a quasi-judicial decision by the USF
              Honor Committee after consideration of the merits at his hearing on May 17, 2024. (Code of Civil
              Procedure, § 1094.5.)                                                 
                  42.  USF proceeded without or in excess of its jurisdiction, and prejudicially abused its
              discretion. USF prejudicially abused its discretion because it did not proceed in the manner required
              by law. (Code of Civil Procedure, § 1094.5(b).)                       
                  43.  USF’s decision must be set aside as it is invalid under Code of Civil Procedure
              section 1094.5, as USF made findings not supported by the evidence, and committed prejudicial
              abuses of discretion in the following ways:                           
                       a.   The decision is conclusory and does not address the fact that Petitioner did
                  not omit citation of the article he used in his paper, his format for attribution was flawed.
                       b.   The USF’s decision does not consider the fact that Petitioner did not dispute
                  his mistake, apologized, and expressed defensible reasons for misinterpreting guidelines and

                                             -8 -                                   
                                                                                    
                    PETITION FOR WRIT OF ADMINISTRATIVE MANDATE, ORDINARY MANDATE,  
                                      AND DECLARATOR RELIEF                         
   .DTL,SETAICOSSA&                                                                 
   SAMIS                                                                            
    WALTASYENROTTA                                                                  
                  rules given to him both verbally and in writing by USF staff.     
                       c.   The USF’s decision is conclusory in that it does not address any of Mr.
                  Dong’s defenses in reaching a decision.                           
                  44.  Petitionerdoes not have a plain, speedy, and adequate remedy in the ordinary course
              of the law.                                                           
                               REEQUEST FOR DECLARATORY  RELIEF                     
                                      (Code Civ. Proc., § 1060)                     
                  1.   Petitioner refer to paragraphs 1 through 44above and incorporates the same by this
              reference as though fully set forth herein at length.                 
                  2.   Petitioner has an actual present controversy with the USF regarding the USF’s
              unlawful and inappropriate enforcement of the Honor Code as described above which prevents
              Petitioner from lawfully conducting his business as a student of the USF School of Law.
                  3.   Petitioner brings this original action for a declaration of the parties’ rights and duties,
              given the USF acted outside the scope of its authority, and seek a declaratory judgment or order
              addressing the following controversies and legal questions:           
                       a. Whether USF has failed to comply with the statutory and regulatory definitions of
                         its statutes and regulations regarding the eligibility of Petitioners to participate in
                         university activities;                                     
                       b. Whether USFhas abused its discretion and acted in excess of its legal authority by
                         suspending Mr. Dong from USF School of Law for the Fall 2024 semester (among
                         the other punitive measures imposed); and                  
                       c. Whether USF has violated the due process rights of Petitioner by misconstruing its
                         regulatory and statutory requirements pertaining to the suspension of students
                         from the USF system, and related issues, and issuing orders to Petitioner forfeit
                         their Art Law course credit and delay their graduation by a full calendar year as a
                         result of the ruling.                                      
                  4.   This declaration is necessary and appropriate at this time so Petitioners may continue
 
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
                                                                                    
           1  to practice their professions without interference.                   
                                                                                    
           2                                PRAYER                                  
                                                                                    
           3  WHEREFORE, PetitionerJialiangDongprays for judgment as follows:       
                                                                                    
           4      1. That the court issue a writ of ordinary mandate under Code of Civil Procedure section
                                                                                    
           5         1085, directed to Respondent, compelling it to set aside its suspension of Mr. Dongand
                                                                                    
           6         his education and requiring him to perform the above-stated actions;
           7      2. That the court issue a writ of administrative mandate under Code of Civil Procedure
                                                                                    
           8         section 1094.5, directed to Respondent, compelling it to set aside the suspension and all
                                                                                    
           9         entries in Mr. Dong’s permanent records and allowing Mr. Dongtoparticipate inUSF
                                                                                    
          10         sanctioned activities;                                         
                                                                                    
          11      3. That the court issue a declaratory judgment deciding those issues outlined above in the
                                                                                    
          12         Request for Declaratory Relief;                                
          13      4. For costs of suit incurred herein,including reasonable fees for preparation of the
                                                                                    
          14         administrative record;                                         
                                                                                    
          15      5. Should the court during or after hearing make a tentative decision to deny the petition,
                                                                                    
          16         Petitionerrequests a written statement ofdecision pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure
                                                                                    
          17         section 632,explaining the factual and legal basis for the decision as to each of the issues
                                                                                    
          18         raised by the pleadings; and,                                  
                                                                                    
          19      6. For such other and further relief as the court deems proper.
